threats.title: Missing claimId Validation in updateTimerCacheByClaim Allows Timer Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: The function updateTimerCacheByClaim allows an attacker to inflate the timer of a malicious edge by using the timer cache of an honest claiming edge, because it fails to verify that the claiming edge actually claims the target edge.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checkClaimIdLink function only verifies that the claiming edge's originId matches the target edge's mutualId. Since rival edges share the same mutualId, an attacker's rival edge can successfully pass this check against an honest party's claiming edge, allowing cross-contamination of timers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing claimId equality check; only mutualId and level are verified

preconditions: An honest edge and a malicious rival edge exist at level L
An honest claiming edge exists at level L+1 claiming the honest edge
The honest claiming edge has accumulated a non-zero timer cache

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Insufficient validation of cross-level edge relationships
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: store.edges[edgeId].mutualId() == store.edges[claimingEdgeId].originId is true for all rivals, allowing an attacker to steal the honest party's timer

analysis_questions: Can the inflated timer cache be propagated up to the root edge?
Does this allow the malicious root edge to be confirmed via confirmEdgeByTime?
validation_requirements: Trace the timer cache propagation from the malicious leaf up to the root
Verify if confirmEdgeByTime will accept the inflated timer

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates a malicious rival edge. Honest party creates a claiming edge on their own honest edge. Attacker calls updateTimerCacheByClaim passing their malicious edge and the honest claiming edge. The malicious edge receives the honest timer, allowing the attacker to win the challenge by time.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of all bridged funds as attacker can confirm invalid state transitions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires Security Council intervention during grace period

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for cross-rival timer inheritance attempts

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Cost of staking one layer zero edge
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after honest claiming edge accumulates time
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as TimerCacheUpdated event for malicious edge

threats.title: Stake Slashing Evasion and Contract Drain via Child Creation on Invalid Branch
threats.hypothesis_description: A losing staker can evade slashing and withdraw their full stake by creating a child assertion on their own invalid branch, making themselves 'inactive' while draining the contract's stake pool.
threats.investigation_rationale: When a sibling assertion is created, requiredStake is transferred to loserStakeEscrow from the contract balance, but the staker's amountStaked is not deducted. The staker can later become inactive by extending their branch and withdraw their undeducted stake.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External transfer to escrow without deducting from staker's internal balance

preconditions: Attacker has sufficient stake to create an assertion
Attacker creates a sibling assertion (not the first child)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State update missing after external transfer
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: amountStaked is not reduced when requiredStake is sent to loserStakeEscrow

analysis_questions: Can the attacker successfully call returnOldDeposit after extending their invalid branch?
Does the contract have sufficient balance from other honest stakers to fund the withdrawal?
validation_requirements: Simulate creating a sibling assertion, extending it, and calling returnOldDeposit

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker stakes and creates a sibling assertion. Contract sends requiredStake to escrow. Attacker uses a second account to create a child on the sibling assertion. Attacker's first account is now considered inactive and calls returnOldDeposit, withdrawing their full stake and stealing from honest validators.
prerequisites: Capital to stake on two assertions

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drain of all honest validators' stakes from the Rollup contract
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, funds are permanently extracted or sent to escrow

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for withdrawing stake after creating a sibling assertion and extending it

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as returnOldDeposit succeeding for a staker who created a sibling

threats.title: Global machineStep Used for Local Merkle Tree Verification Causes One-Step Proofs to Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: The confirmEdgeByOneStepProof function incorrectly passes the global machineStep as the index to verifyInclusionProof for SmallStep Merkle trees, causing valid proofs to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: SmallStep Merkle trees are built using local heights (0 to LAYERZERO_SMALLSTEPEDGE_HEIGHT). Passing the global machineStep (which accumulates across all levels) as the leaf index will cause the Merkle verification to fail for any step other than the very first step of the block.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Passing global accumulator (machineStep) to a function expecting a local index

preconditions: A dispute reaches a length-1 SmallStep edge
The global machineStep is greater than the local startHeight

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: verifyInclusionProof uses machineStep instead of store.edges[edgeId].startHeight
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Use of local height for Merkle verification

analysis_questions: Does verifyInclusionProof strictly require the index to match the tree's construction index?
Are SmallStep trees definitively built using local heights?
validation_requirements: Trace the construction of startHistoryRoot for SmallStep edges
Attempt to execute confirmEdgeByOneStepProof with a non-zero global offset

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An honest validator bisects a dispute down to a single step and submits a valid one-step proof. The transaction reverts because the Merkle verification fails due to the index mismatch. The honest validator cannot confirm their edge, allowing the attacker to win by time.
prerequisites: A dispute that does not occur at the exact first step of the block

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Breakdown of the fraud proof system, allowing invalid assertions to finalize
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix the index variable

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests for one-step proofs at non-zero global offsets

threats.title: removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk Can Be Triggered During Protocol Pause
threats.hypothesis_description: The validator whitelist can be permanently disabled by any user if the protocol is paused by the Security Council for longer than VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _validatorIsAfk check relies on block.number, which continues to increase while the contract is paused. The removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk function lacks the whenNotPaused modifier, allowing it to be executed during or immediately after a pause.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing whenNotPaused modifier on state-changing security function

preconditions: Protocol is paused for >= VALIDATOR_AFK_BLOCKS (201600 blocks)

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: whenNotPaused modifier or pause duration accounting

analysis_questions: Can removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk be called while the contract is paused?
Does disabling the whitelist introduce immediate risks upon unpausing?
validation_requirements: Simulate a pause of 201600 blocks and attempt to call the function

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The Security Council pauses the rollup during an incident. After 28 days, an attacker calls removeWhitelistAfterValidatorAfk. The whitelist is permanently disabled. When the protocol is unpaused, anyone can immediately participate as a validator, potentially spamming the network.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but degrades protocol security posture
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to re-enable whitelist

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for AFK timeout during paused state

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: During or immediately after extended pause
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as successful transaction disabling whitelist

threats.title: fastConfirmNewAssertion Transfers Uncollected Stake Causing Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: If the anyTrustFastConfirmer creates a sibling assertion via fastConfirmNewAssertion, the contract transfers requiredStake to loserStakeEscrow without pulling tokens from the caller, draining the contract's balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike newStakeOnNewAssertion, fastConfirmNewAssertion does not call receiveTokens. If it triggers the sibling assertion path, it uses the contract's existing balance (belonging to other stakers) to fund the escrow transfer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External transfer of funds without corresponding inbound transfer

preconditions: Assertion created is not the first child

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: safeTransfer to loserStakeEscrow uses contract balance without pulling from msg.sender
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Skip escrow transfer for fast confirmer, or pull tokens

analysis_questions: Is the fast confirmer expected to ever create sibling assertions?
Does the contract have sufficient balance to survive this transfer?
validation_requirements: Simulate fastConfirmNewAssertion creating a sibling and check contract balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised AnyTrust committee calls fastConfirmNewAssertion to create a sibling assertion. The contract transfers requiredStake to the escrow. Honest validators are subsequently unable to withdraw their stakes due to insufficient contract balance.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires DAO to return funds from loserStakeEscrow

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for fastConfirmNewAssertion creating sibling assertions

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as unexpected transfer to loserStakeEscrow

threats.title: fastConfirmAssertion Bypasses All Security Checks and Challenge Periods
threats.hypothesis_description: The fastConfirmAssertion function allows the anyTrustFastConfirmer to instantly confirm any pending assertion, bypassing the confirmPeriodBlocks, sequential confirmation checks, and challenge outcomes.
threats.investigation_rationale: This function is a privileged backdoor intended for AnyTrust chains. If the AnyTrust committee's keys are compromised, an attacker can instantly confirm a malicious state transition and drain the L1 bridge.
deployment_assumptions: anyTrustFastConfirmer is set to a non-zero address

proof_of_signal.pattern: Privileged function bypassing core protocol invariants

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Calls confirmAssertionInternal directly without deadline or challenge checks
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: None, works as designed but carries high risk

analysis_questions: Is the anyTrustFastConfirmer role adequately protected by a multisig?
Can the Security Council intervene if this function is abused?
validation_requirements: Verify the deployment configuration of anyTrustFastConfirmer

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker compromises the AnyTrust committee keys, creates a malicious assertion stealing all bridged funds, and immediately calls fastConfirmAssertion. The assertion is confirmed instantly, and the attacker withdraws the funds.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of all bridged assets
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None once funds are withdrawn from L1 bridge
